name: Young FMT improved old mouse frailty, grip strength, and metabolic markers
summary: A 2025 old-mouse study adds recent functional and metabolomic support for young-to-old microbiota replacement.
statement: Old male mice receiving young microbiota had higher alpha diversity than old-to-old controls, reduced body weight and fat accumulation, lower frailty, increased grip strength, improved depression- and anxiety-like behavior, lower age-related long-chain fatty acids, higher amino acids, lower amygdala inflammatory pathways, and higher oxidative-phosphorylation signaling.
population_or_model: Old male mice receiving young or old donor microbiota
time_horizon: Two months after FMT in old-to-old, young-to-old, and young-to-young groups
quantitative_note: The PubMed figure legend reports 15 male mice per group and physical-function testing before and after two months of FMT; the abstract reports direction but not numeric effect sizes.
caveats: The study is in male mice and needs replication across sex, age, donor, and facility conditions.
Body composition, behavior, and metabolic changes are preclinical and do not establish human aging efficacy.
Young-to-old FMT may not have the same engraftment or safety profile in older adults.
